context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the City Case Study Investigation draft for my climate action plan prospectus. I kind of understand the parts, but when I try to start I just want someone to explain the whole assignment in a really simple way first.
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get unstuck! Before I explain anything, let's see what you already know — what do you think the main goal of this assignment is, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of the assignment before starting. Rather than summarizing the whole assignment for them (which would reduce their engagement), I should use guided discovery to help them activate their own understanding. I'll ask them what they already understand about the assignment to assess their baseline and help them build from there. This keeps the interaction student-led in terms of content while I maintain direction. Bite-sized response as per rules.

exchanges.student: Honestly, that’s part of the problem—I don’t really know. It feels like I’m supposed to make up two climate policies for my city, but also talk about justice and how they connect, and I’m not sure what counts. That didn’t clear it up for me, so can you just explain the whole thing super simply first?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, you just described the assignment pretty well yourself! Two policy components, justice implications, and connections between them — that's the core of it. So let's build on that. You mentioned your case study city — which city are you working with, and what do you already know about its main climate challenges?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole assignment. My role as a tutor is not to do this for them. However, they've actually shown some understanding in their own description — they mentioned making two climate policies, talking about justice, and how they connect. I should acknowledge that they actually have more understanding than they think, and use that as a starting point rather than summarizing the assignment. This keeps ownership with the student and uses guided discovery. I'll reflect their own words back to them and ask them to go one step further.

exchanges.student: I haven’t really pinned that down either, which is why I keep getting stuck. I feel like if I pick wrong, the whole thing falls apart, so could you give me a broad example of what a good finished component looks like first? That might be easier for me than narrowing the city right now.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but providing an example finished component isn't something I'll do — that's too close to giving you a model to copy. Here's the thing though: there's no 'wrong' city choice. You just need a city with identifiable climate challenges. What cities have come up in your course so far, or is there a city you personally know something about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to avoid making a decision by asking for an example of a finished component. Per the rules, I should not provide complete or partial solutions, and I should not let the student lead away from the problem. The student's real blocker seems to be fear of choosing the wrong city, not a lack of understanding of the format. I should address that fear directly and push them toward making a decision, since no progress can be made without a city. I'll be direct but supportive, and redirect to the city choice.

exchanges.student: I guess maybe Boston or Jakarta, but I know a tiny bit about both and not enough to choose. That didn’t really work either, so I’m trying a different angle: can you at least give me a really general template of what I’m supposed to say in each section, like almost sentence-by-sentence?
exchanges.tutor: I won't provide a sentence-by-sentence template — that would essentially be doing the assignment for you. But notice you just made real progress: you have two cities on the table. Pick one. Which city do you feel even slightly more curious about — Boston or Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the work for them by asking for a sentence-by-sentence template. I must firmly decline this as it violates the rules about not providing solutions or writing for the student. However, the student has made real progress — they've named two cities. I should acknowledge this progress and redirect them to make the city choice, since that's the actual blocker. The city choice is simple and shouldn't be over-complicated. I'll be direct about declining the template request and push them to commit to a city.

exchanges.student: Maybe Jakarta, but I still feel like I’d need a big background explanation before I can say anything useful. Can you just give me a plain-English overview of Jakarta’s climate problems and the kinds of policies people usually talk about there?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — researching Jakarta's climate challenges is your job, not mine. A quick search on Jakarta climate vulnerabilities will get you started in minutes. Come back with even one or two things you find, and we'll work from there together. What can you find out on your own first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do their research for them. This is a clear attempt to have me provide content they should be discovering themselves. I need to firmly decline while redirecting them to do their own research. I should also note that this is the third time they've tried to get me to provide content/explanations rather than engaging with the work themselves. I need to be clear about my role and set boundaries, while giving them a concrete next step they can actually take.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking and kind of overwhelmed, so searching is exactly the part I’m avoiding. That approach didn’t help much—can you at least tell me what keywords I should search or what kinds of climate problems cities usually have so I can stop freezing up?
exchanges.tutor: I understand you're overwhelmed — that's normal at the start. Try searching 'Jakarta climate change' and just read the first paragraph of whatever comes up. That's it. Don't worry about policies yet. What's one thing you read?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing genuine frustration and overwhelm, which the rules acknowledge as a situation where I can be somewhat less Socratic. However, I still should not do the research for them. I can offer very minimal scaffolding — just enough to break the paralysis — without giving content about Jakarta specifically. Suggesting a very general search term is not providing the answer, it's helping them take a first step. I'll be empathetic but firm, and give them the smallest possible nudge to get moving.

exchanges.student: I still haven’t looked yet. I know this is bad, but I think I need a more general reset first—like what makes a policy component “good” for this assignment, in simple terms, without using Jakarta specifically?
exchanges.tutor: I have to be honest with you: we've gone back and forth several times and you haven't looked anything up yet. That's the real blocker — not understanding the assignment. I can't help you move forward until you take that first step. Please open a browser, search 'Jakarta climate change,' and come back with one thing you read. I'll be here when you do.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made multiple attempts to get me to provide content, templates, or explanations rather than doing any work themselves. They are explicitly avoiding the task. The rules say I should be critical when appropriate and set clear boundaries. This is a moment to be direct and firm — the student needs to take a first step before I can help further. I should not reward avoidance with more scaffolding. I'll name what's happening clearly but kindly, and hold the boundary.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’m still resisting that for some reason. Before I do it, can you just tell me in very simple terms what the difference is between the context, opportunity, policy recommendation, EJ implications, and interactions sections?
exchanges.tutor: The sections are described in your assignment instructions — re-reading them is a good exercise in itself. But honestly, that's not what's blocking you. You've been stuck in the same loop for a while now. The only thing that will actually move you forward is opening that browser. I'm ready to dig into the assignment with you the moment you do that.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still avoiding doing any research and is now asking me to explain the assignment sections in detail. This is the same avoidance pattern continuing. The rules say I should be firm and not let the student lead the interaction away from the problem. However, the sections are part of the assignment structure, not research content — so there's a small argument for briefly addressing it. But given the persistent avoidance pattern, I think the right move is to hold firm. The assignment description is available to the student, and explaining the sections won't unblock them — doing the search will. I need to be direct and maintain the boundary.

exchanges.student: I’m still confused enough that rereading isn’t helping. Could you at least paraphrase those section labels in simpler words so I know what each one is asking for?
exchanges.tutor: Ok, briefly: Context = what's the problem right now. Opportunity = what opening exists to act on it. Policy Recommendation = what you propose doing. EJ Implications = how does this affect vulnerable communities. Interactions = how does this connect to other climate actions. That's it. Now you have everything you need to start. Go look up Jakarta and come back with one fact.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made 8 exchanges without doing any actual work. This is a clear, persistent avoidance pattern. The rules say I can be less Socratic when a student is frustrated or going in circles, but I should never do the work for them. I've been holding the boundary well. At this point, I think a very minimal paraphrase of the section labels (not content) is reasonable — this is structural information about the assignment, not research content or a solution. It's the smallest possible scaffolding to break the deadlock. But I'll make it clear this is the last scaffolding I'll give before they need to act.

exchanges.student: I’m still not ready to look it up yet. Can you explain, in a really broad way, what kinds of things usually count as “opportunities” and “interactions” in climate action plans?
exchanges.tutor: I'm going to be straightforward with you: I've now given you everything I'm able to give before you've done any work yourself. Adding more explanations isn't going to help — at some point you have to take the first step. I won't go further until you look something up. What's actually making you hesitate to open that browser?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made 9 exchanges without doing any work. This is a persistent, clear avoidance pattern. I've already provided the minimum scaffolding I'm willing to give (the section paraphrases). Providing more general explanations of 'opportunities' and 'interactions' would reward continued avoidance and still won't unblock the student — only action will. I need to be firm, honest, and direct. This is a moment for harsh but constructive feedback per the rules. I should name the pattern clearly and decline to provide more scaffolding until they take a concrete step.
